Details:
A bench-scale study [2635] using ozone, followed by increasing pH to 11, followed by hydrogen peroxide addition consistently achieved reductions (14 percent to greater than 97 percent) of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs), including perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluoropentane sulfonate (PFPeS), and fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2). However, under some treatment conditions, concentrations of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), and perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS) increased. The study did not test the treatment process at natural pH or evaluate whether treatment at very high pH would be practical for use in larger-scale drinking water applications. 
Other studies [2160, 2647, 2847] found removal of PFASs from water using ozone and hydrogen peroxide to be ineffective. One bench-scale study [2647], using an ozone dose of 1 mg/L and an unspecified hydrogen peroxide dose, found 4 to 13 percent removal of PFASs, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, and perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA). Another bench-scale study [2160] achieved only 2 percent removal of PFOS with an applied ozone dose of 2.6 grams per hour and hydrogen peroxide dose of 3.0 mg/L. A pilot-scale study [2847] using an ozone dose of 15 mg/L and a hydrogen peroxide dose of 21.2 mg/L observed a 2 percent increase in the concentration of PFOS and a 5 percent increase in the concentration of PFOA.
